# t20.2 - Boot DB2 driver/runtime wiring for the primary infra lane

## Summary

Implemented the missing Spring Boot 3 DB2 runtime wiring for the packaged WAR so the primary
infrastructure lane can advance past classpath/bootstrap failures. The build now packages the IBM
DB2 JDBC driver, upgrades Flyway to a DB2-capable line, includes the Derby and DB2 Flyway database
plugins, and auto-adds the DB2 migration location whenever the datasource URL or driver points at
DB2.

The DB2 startup probe now reaches Hikari/Flyway connectivity and fails only because no DB2 server
is listening at `127.0.0.1:50000` in this task environment. This closes the backend-owned wiring
gap from `t20.1`; container availability and reachable DB2 runtime remain owned by `t20.3`.

## Changes

- Added `com.ibm.db2:jcc:11.5.9.0` as a runtime dependency so the repackaged Boot WAR carries the
  DB2 JDBC driver required by the primary infra lane.
- Upgraded Flyway to `10.22.0` and added `flyway-database-derby` plus `flyway-database-db2` so the
  migrated app can continue to validate Derby locally and also bootstrap against DB2.
- Removed the hard-coded Derby driver from `application.yml` so Spring Boot can auto-detect the
  correct JDBC driver from the datasource URL/runtime classpath.
- Added `FlywayDatasourcePlatformConfiguration` so DB2 datasources automatically append
  `classpath:db/migration/db2` without changing the Derby default path.
- Added a focused unit test for DB2 detection and Flyway-location customization.

## Downstream Notes

- `t20.4` can now reuse the tester's DB2-backed startup command without the prior
  `Cannot load driver class: com.ibm.db2.jcc.DB2Driver` failure.
- `t20.3` still needs to provide a reachable DB2 container/listener and any environment variables
  used to point the packaged WAR at that container.

## Test Results

- Command: `cmd /d /c "set JAVA_HOME=%USERPROFILE%\scoop\apps\microsoft17-jdk\current&& set PATH=%JAVA_HOME%\bin;%PATH%&& mvn -Dtest=FlywayDatasourcePlatformConfigurationTest test"`
- Command: `cmd /d /c "set JAVA_HOME=%USERPROFILE%\scoop\apps\microsoft17-jdk\current&& set PATH=%JAVA_HOME%\bin;%PATH%&& mvn -Dmaven.test.skip=true package"`
- Command: `$env:JAVA_HOME = "$env:USERPROFILE\scoop\apps\microsoft17-jdk\current"; $env:Path = "$env:JAVA_HOME\bin;" + $env:Path; $env:SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL = 'jdbc:db2://127.0.0.1:50000/tradedb'; $env:SPRING_DATASOURCE_DRIVER_CLASS_NAME = 'com.ibm.db2.jcc.DB2Driver'; $env:SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME = 'db2inst1'; $env:SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD = 'password'; java -jar target\io.openliberty.sample.daytrader8.war --server.port=19095`
- Command: `cmd /d /c "set JAVA_HOME=%USERPROFILE%\scoop\apps\microsoft17-jdk\current&& set PATH=%JAVA_HOME%\bin;%PATH%&& mvn -Dtest=TradeConfigControllerTest,RestAccessBoundaryTest,MarketSummaryHandshakeInterceptorTest,AuthenticationApplicationServiceTest,AccountProfileDataBeanTest,MarketSummaryPublisherTest,FlywayDatasourcePlatformConfigurationTest test"`
- Passed: 4
- Failed: 0
- Skipped: 0
- Verification details:
  - The focused DB2 config test passed (`3/3`).
  - The packaged WAR rebuilt successfully with the new DB2/Flyway runtime classpath.
  - The DB2 startup probe advanced past driver loading and Flyway DB2-module resolution, then hit
    the expected environment-level `Connection refused` against `127.0.0.1:50000`, which confirms
    the backend wiring gap is closed and the remaining blocker is external DB2 availability.
  - The existing backend regression slice passed (`20/20`).
